texts.label: text
texts.orig: Post combustion carbon dioxide capture raises tremendous chemical engineering challenges. For the first generation of industrial installations, gas liquid absorption in chemical solvents is classically considered to be the best available technology. Two major bottlenecks have however to be solved in order to achieve technico-economical targets: decrease the energy requirement of the process (e.g. through novel solvents or heat integration approaches) and decrease the size of the installation (through process intensification).
texts.text: Post combustion carbon dioxide capture raises tremendous chemical engineering challenges. For the first generation of industrial installations, gas liquid absorption in chemical solvents is classically considered to be the best available technology. Two major bottlenecks have however to be solved in order to achieve technico-economical targets: decrease the energy requirement of the process (e.g. through novel solvents or heat integration approaches) and decrease the size of the installation (through process intensification).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study intends to explore the possibilities and limitations of membrane contactors, which are considered as one of the most promising strategy for intensified CO2 capture by gas-liquid absorption. A very large number of studies is continuously reported on this topic, including materials, mass transfer or process design issues, but a rigorous evaluation of their effective potential in terms of intensification is still lacking. Moreover, controversial results have been reported such as intensification factors, compared to packed columns, ranging between 10 and 0.8 on a total unit volume basis.
texts.text: This study intends to explore the possibilities and limitations of membrane contactors, which are considered as one of the most promising strategy for intensified CO2 capture by gas-liquid absorption. A very large number of studies is continuously reported on this topic, including materials, mass transfer or process design issues, but a rigorous evaluation of their effective potential in terms of intensification is still lacking. Moreover, controversial results have been reported such as intensification factors, compared to packed columns, ranging between 10 and 0.8 on a total unit volume basis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This unclear situation results from different factors. First, experimental comparison of membrane contactors vs. packed absorption columns performances is indeed seldom. Second, the evaluation of membrane contactors is systematically performed at laboratory scale, under operating conditions which do not necessarily reflect industrial operation (i.e. fresh amine solutions are used, limited capture ratio are achieved). These simplifying assumptions have obviously to be reconsidered if a realistic comparison for industrial operation is aimed. More importantly, pressure drop levels, which are known to be very small for packed columns (typically 50 mBar on the gas side for an industrial packed column), have to be considered in order to minimize the energy impact of the process. An analysis combining intensification and pressure drop aspects for membrane contactors design, with solvent flowing inside the fibers, and the associated trade-off, which, to our knowledge, has not been achieved for CO2 absorption, is presented based on experimental and simulation results. Practical guidelines on the set of conditions for membrane materials (i.e. permeability and thickness), fiber geometry (external diameter, thickness) and module design (length, packing factor) which enable a significant process intensification effect are finally proposed.

Similarly, a very large number of studies are dedicated to design the most efficient carbon dioxide capture process, because it represents 60-80% of the cost of the overall CCS chain (Davidson and Metz, 2005). For a post-combustion CO2 capture situation, gas-liquid absorption in a chemical solvent, schematically shown in Fig. 1, is considered today as the best available technology and several demonstration units based on this separation process already exist or have been projected in the next years (Steeneveldt et al., 2006). The cost of the capture step remains however too high in order to enable a massive diffusion of the technology. A decrease by a factor 2-3 is needed, which represents a major engineering challenge.

texts.text: Basically, a significant decrease of the cost of the gas absorption capture step can be achieved thanks to two main improvements:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -a lower energy requirement, in order to generate lower operating costs (OPEX). The currently energy requirement for a post-combustion capture process can be estimated around 3.5 GJ per ton of CO2 (Steeneveldt et al., 2006). The target of the E.U. is 2 GJ per ton (Deschamps and Pilavachi, 2004). Intense research activities are carried out in order to identify novel solvents which would offer a high selectivity towards CO2, a high capacity, a fast kinetics together with a minimal energy for regeneration compared to the first generation of amine solvents (especially compared to MonoEthanolAmine, MEA, which is classically taken as the baseline situation).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane contactors are often considered to be one of the most promising strategies in order to achieve intensified CO2 capture by gas-liquid absorption (Feron et al., 1992). The basic concept of a membrane contactor has been already discussed in numerous papers (Gabelman and Hwang, 1999; Li and Chen, 2005) and it will be briefly exposed hereafter (Fig. 2). The key idea is to make use of a porous, hydrophobic membrane material which separates the gas and the liquid flows in a gas absorption process. It is obvious that a supplementary mass transfer resistance (namely due to the membrane) has to be considered in that case, compared to a classical gas-liquid absorption situation where a direct contact between the two phases takes place. Nevertheless, the possibility to develop an extremely high interfacial area ( a ) thanks to the membrane, compared to classical gas-liquid equipments, is expected to significantly counterbalance the decrease of the overall mass transfer coefficient ( K ). As a consequence, much higher effective mass transfer performances (which depend on K · a ) can possibly be obtained, thus leading to an intensification effect (Qi and Cussler, 1985).

As a consequence, much higher effective mass transfer performances (which depend on K · a ) can possibly be obtained, thus leading to an intensification effect (Qi and Cussler, 1985).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the more specific case of carbon dioxide capture, numerous studies have been reported on the performances of membrane contactors, most often with MEA solutions as a model solvent (Li and Chen, 2005). A 'liquid in' configuration (i.e. solvent flowing inside the fibers) is almost systematically chosen for this application (Mansourizadeh and Ismail, 2009); nevertheless, it should be noted that a rational analysis of the 'liquid in/gas out' vs. 'liquid out/gas in' strategy is not reported, to our knowledge. A great number of membrane materials, hollow fiber geometries and module configurations have been explored and, globally speaking, the mass transfer improvement remains the most important indicator which is investigated. Unfortunately, the situation is rather unclear in terms of the effective intensification factor ( I ) which can be practically achieved for this particular case. The effective intensification factor can be expressed, for a given set of operating conditions, as the relative volumetric absorption capacity of the process; more specifically, it can be calculated as the number of moles of CO2 which are effectively absorbed in the liquid solvent per unit volume of installation per second (mol CO2 m -3 s -1 ), divided by the performances of a packed column operated under similar conditions. This definition obviously addresses the question of the choice of a reference case; fortunately, for post combustion CO2 capture by absorption in a packed column fed by a 30 wt% MEA solution, numerous reports and scientific papers have been reported (Abu-Zahra et al., 2007; Tobiesen et al., 2007; NETL, 2007; Mangalapally and Hasse, 2010) and baseline conditions in terms of the flue gas composition, temperature and pressure of the absorbers, inlet and outlet solvent composition, capture ratio and dimension of the unit can be proposed. The values of these different parameters for packed columns (at industrial scale) and membrane contactors (which are limited, up to now, at laboratory scale studies) are listed in Table 1. It can be seen that the average volumetric absorption performances of a classical packed column can be estimated around 1 mol CO2 m -3 s -1 . Consequently, this value will be taken as the reference case in this study and membrane contactor performances will be compared to this data in terms of process intensification.

texts.orig: Generally speaking, a rigorous evaluation of the effective intensification potential of membrane contactors compared to packed columns is still lacking; to our knowledge, apart from one literature source (deMontigny et al., 2005) which concludes that membrane contactor enable to attain a mass transfer intensification factor around 4, other reports remain limited to membrane contactors only, without a lucid and critical comparison to packed columns. texts.orig: A series of experiments has been performed in a first step, in order to check the consistency of the modeling framework. In a second step, numerous simulations and a parametric study was performed. The effective intensification potential of membrane contactors for CO2 absorption in MEA process, under realistic operating conditions, is finally proposed, based on an analysis which incorporates pressure drop constraints. Practical guidelines in terms of membrane materials (gas permeance), fiber preparation (diameter, thickness) and hollow fiber module design (fiber packing fraction and associated length) which fulfill the previous criteria will finally be proposed.

texts.orig: 2. Materials and methods
texts.text: 2. Materials and methods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two membrane contactors (effective length 0.24 m), consisting of a 54 fibers bundle into a 0.013 m internal diameter module, have been tested on the experimental gas absorption set-up described in Fig. 3. These two modules have been selected in order to cover a range of membrane characteristics (thickness and mass transfer resistance), as suggested by previous studies (Al-Safar et al., 1997). For the first membrane contactor, noted PP, a thin (50 𝛍 m thickness, 380 𝛍 m external diameter), polypropylene (PP) microporous hollow fiber membrane (Membrana Oxyphan) was selected in order to explore the low membrane mass transfer resistance case; for the second membrane contactor, noted PDMS, a thick (165 𝛍 m thickness, 635 𝛍 m external diameter) polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) dense hollow fiber membrane (Silastic, Dow Corning) was chosen in order to evaluate membrane contactors showing a higher membrane mass transfer resistance.
texts.text: Two membrane contactors (effective length 0.24 m), consisting of a 54 fibers bundle into a 0.013 m internal diameter module, have been tested on the experimental gas absorption set-up described in Fig. 3. These two modules have been selected in order to cover a range of membrane characteristics (thickness and mass transfer resistance), as suggested by previous studies (Al-Safar et al., 1997). For the first membrane contactor, noted PP, a thin (50 𝛍 m thickness, 380 𝛍 m external diameter), polypropylene (PP) microporous hollow fiber membrane (Membrana Oxyphan) was selected in order to explore the low membrane mass transfer resistance case; for the second membrane contactor, noted PDMS, a thick (165 𝛍 m thickness, 635 𝛍 m external diameter) polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) dense hollow fiber membrane (Silastic, Dow Corning) was chosen in order to evaluate membrane contactors showing a higher membrane mass transfer resistance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For each experiment, CO2/N2 mixtures (5-30 vol% CO2) were prepared thanks to two mass flow controllers (model 5800S, Brooks) and flowed on the shell side of the membrane contactor. A MEA aqueous solution (20-30 wt%), prepared by dissolution of high purity MEA (Sigma Aldrich Reagent grade, purity > 99%) in distilled water, flowed inside the fibers countercurrently. The liquid flowrate was controlled by a high precision pump (HPLC type 515, waters). The hollow fiber module was installed in a thermostated oven and an infrared analyzer (MGA-3000 Multi-gas analyzer ADC) was used in order to measure the gas phase composition at the entrance and at the exit of the module. Care was taken to achieve steady state conditions for each measurement, reflected by a constant exit CO2 concentration on the gas side over time. For each module, different sets of conditions were tested (gas and liquid flowrates) in order to obtain a CO2 capture ratio ( R ) ranging between 10 and 90%. The main target of these experiments was to evaluate the capability of the modeling approach, which is detailed in the next section, to correctly predict membrane contactors performances in two different cases (PP and PDMS modules).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The possibility of the modeling approach detailed in the previous section to predict real CO2 absorption performances of membrane contactors has been first evaluated. A series of experimental carbon dioxide capture ratios ( R ) obtained with the two different membrane contactors (PP and PDMS) is compared to the model predictions in Fig. 5. In that case, L is known and fixed, and the capture ratio ( R ) is tuned by changing the inlet gas flowrate ( uG ). The only parameter which cannot be precisely predicted and imposed in the model calculation is the membrane mass transfer coefficient km . Consequently, simulations have been performed with km taken as the only adjustable parameter. A value of 2.1 × 10 -5 m s -1 has been obtained for the PDMS module, which is consistent to CO2 gas permeability data of silicone rubber (Stern et al., 1987). texts.orig: 4.2. Parametric study: intensification performances of membrane contactors
texts.text: 4.2. Parametric study: intensification performances of membrane contactors

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a first step, a series of 21 000 simulations has been performed in order to cover the range of variation of the 5 variables listed in Table 3. The final module length ( L ), intensification factor ( I ), gas pressure drop ( /Delta1 PG ) and liquid pressure drop ( /Delta1 PL ) corresponding to each set of conditions have been gathered in a master file. The distribution of the intensification factor I (i.e. the volumetric absorption capacity of a membrane contactor divided by the average volumetric absorption capacity of a packed column) is shown in Fig. 6. It can be noted first that an intensification effect (i.e. I > 1) is far to be systematic. A significant ratio of the simulations leads indeed to intensification factors I below unity (typically 30% of the data set). The maximum intensification factor that can be obtained in the range of variables investigated through these computations is impressive (around 52 for the best data set). A more refined selection procedure is however necessary among this raw data set, taking into account simulation output variables (module length, pressure drop on the liquid and gas phase), in order to select only realistic conditions. This point is discussed afterwards. Interestingly, a large ratio of the simulation results (around 40%) fall into the range of volumetric performance index which has been previously reported with membrane contactors for CO2 absorption in MEA, with intensification factor values between 2 and 10.

texts.orig: From the mass transfer conditions point of view, a large majority of the simulations correspond to a Hatta number larger than 3 on the overall length of the contactor. Consequently, CO2 absorption essentially takes place at the gas-liquid interface in the different cases covered and the role of liquid hydrodynamic distribution (i.e. solvent velocity profile in the hollow fiber) can be expected to play a minor role. Based on this observation, the 1D approach taken in this study can be considered as adequate. This condition is far to be systematic in membrane contactor operation, but it may be valid here given the value of the Hatta number for the different simulation data sets which have been explored. Additionally, the Graetz number remains almost systematically high enough so that fully developed hydrodynamic conditions are assumed to hold.

texts.orig: Generally speaking, apart from the intensification factor ( I ) discussed above, the final module length ( L ), the gas pressure drop ( /Delta1 PG ) and liquid pressure drop ( /Delta1 PL ) show very broad variation ranges. For example, module lengths vary between 10 -3 and more than 10 4 m (these two extremes logically addressing numerous questions). The gas pressure drop can be as low as 247 Pa (for low gas velocities and low packing factor), but data above 10 7 Pa are obtained in the opposite situation. Similarly, huge, unrealistic (i.e. >10 38 Pa) liquid pressure drops are obtained for very long modules made of low internal diameter membranes (with a low membrane mass transfer coefficient).

texts.orig: A complete parameter sensitivity analysis is beyond the scope of this study. Nevertheless, a limited parameter sensitivity has been performed around a base case and the main results are shown in Fig. 7. The role of membrane mass transfer coefficient km , membrane thickness ı and module packing factor ϕ has been explored in order to point out the influence of the membrane material, hollow fiber and module characteristics, respectively, through a key variable. Different patterns are obtained for each of the three parameters in terms of influence on the intensification factor. The membrane mass transfer coefficient (Fig. 7a) logically plays a major role, and shows continuous improvement of I when km increases, all other parameters being kept constant. More specifically, it is important to note that the achievement of an intensification factor larger than one demands a km value larger than 2 × 10 -3 m s -1 (whatever the values of the other parameters). A short survey of experimental data obtained on different materials for membrane contactor applications shows values between 10 -4 and 5 × 10 -3 m s -1 (Nguyen et al., 2010); this observation can be seen as one explicating factor of the large differences of intensification factors which are experimentally obtained. Apart from the membrane mass transfer coefficient, a thinner membrane wall logically systematically improves the intensification factor (Fig. 7b). From a module design point of view, a larger packing fraction also improves the intensification factor (Fig. 7c). This positive effect can however be counterbalanced by unacceptable pressure drop characteristics, as discussed later.

texts.orig: been proposed in order to take into account the limitations of transmembrane pressure difference. It is indeed crucial to maintain non wetting conditions for membrane contactor operation and a maximal breakthrough pressure has to be imperatively respected for each membrane. The maximal transmembrane pressure which can be tolerated obviously depends on the membrane material characteristics (pore size distribution, contact angle), but 1 Bar can be considered as a standard limit for the membrane materials classically employed for membrane contactor applications (Gabelman and Hwang, 1999). Contrarily to gas phase pressure drop, the comparison of the energy requirement for liquid circulation in packed columns and membrane contactors is not straightforward; for example, an overall energy requirement for pumps of 0.11 GJ per ton of CO2 is mentioned (Steeneveldt et al., 2006) for carbon capture by gas absorption units, but the sharing between the absorption and regeneration units is not detailed. A more refined analysis of the energy requirement associated to liquid pressure drop considerations in membrane contactors would certainly be of interest, but we deliberately restricted our selection procedure to the 1 Bar limit; we think that this limit allows to identify which conditions may respect non wetting constraints without generating an unacceptable energy requirement for liquid circulation purposes.

texts.orig: 5. Practical guidelines for membrane materials, hollow fibers and modules
texts.text: 5. Practical guidelines for membrane materials, hollow fibers and modules

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The practical guidelines for membrane materials, hollow fibers and modules which have been identified to enable an intensification factor to be attained, within the range of variation which have been chosen for this parametric study, can be summarized as follows:
texts.text: The practical guidelines for membrane materials, hollow fibers and modules which have been identified to enable an intensification factor to be attained, within the range of variation which have been chosen for this parametric study, can be summarized as follows:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (1) The most significant and stringent parameter clearly corresponds to the effective membrane mass transfer coefficient km . This conclusion could obviously have been anticipated, but this study shows that a minimal km value of 2 × 10 -3 m s -1 is required if an intensification factor larger than 2 is aimed. Such a challenging constraint has not been quantitatively identified up to now and it corresponds to the upper range of reported data based on representative CO2 absorption experiments. A high porosity, low tortuosity, thin membrane material, simultaneously showing a high hydrophobicity (in order to offer a high enough water breakthrough pressure) is absolutely necessary in order to attain this threshold value. Membrane material screening studies for CO2 absorption applications should be undertaken based on this selection criterion. It is also important to stress at this point that wetting and/or membrane degradation is a key potential problem of membrane contactors which can drastically affect the effective membrane mass transfer coefficient kM (Lv et al., 2010). The extended wetting in the membrane is controlled by the physical properties of the membrane but also by degradation products within, particularly SOx and NOx and oxygen that form organic acids and may change the degree of wetting. As the liquid penetrates the membrane the resistance indeed goes up by orders of magnitude due to the difference between gas and liquid diffusion coefficients. The occurrence of wetting is somehow implicitly included in our analysis since wetting will simply translate into a significant decrease of the effective membrane mass transfer coefficient ( kM ). Consequently, the guidelines on kM which are reported above apply to the effective mass transfer coefficient of the membrane under operating conditions (i.e. taking into account partial wetting). In that context, the 2 × 10 -3 m s -1 target appears to be extremely challenging.

texts.orig: (3) Finally, the module lengths potentially leading to an intensification factor I of 2 or more fall within a very broad range (10 -3 m to 6 m). The extremes limits can obviously be considered as unrealistic; a large ratio of results corresponds however to the typical range of commercially available membrane contactor lengths (0.1-1 m). A fiber volume fraction (module packing) of 0.6 or more is required for the most favorable cases ( I > 6). These observations lead again to practical recommendations for module design. Questions can be addressed on the possible hydrodynamic dispersion complications that may arise for large, industrial scale, membrane contactor modules. These aspects have not been investigated in detail up to now and this study should incite to explore these issues.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Conclusion
texts.text: 6. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study intended to explore the possibilities and limitations of membrane contactors for the intensified capture of CO2 from a post combustion flue gas by absorption in aqueous MEA solutions. A classical membrane contactor modeling approach has been first proposed and successfully checked against a limited series of experimental data. In a second step, a large number of simulations has been performed based on this model in order to evaluate the different design characteristics (module length, packing factor) and the associated operating parameters (gas and liquid pressure drops) which enable a 90% CO2 capture ratio to be obtained on a flue gas containing 15% CO2. The possibility to potentially achieve intensification factors (i.e. relative volume of the unit compared to a standard packed column) up to a factor 11 has been verified. The conditions leading to this level of performances are however very specific and a great number of solutions do not provide an intensification effect or lead to unrealistic gas and/or liquid pressure drops. In order to extract the solutions which are physically and industrially feasible, a selection among the data set which offer an intensification factor ( I > 1), together with a low enough pressure drop on the gas side (<5000 Pa) and on liquid side (<10 5 Pa) has been performed.
texts.text: This study intended to explore the possibilities and limitations of membrane contactors for the intensified capture of CO2 from a post combustion flue gas by absorption in aqueous MEA solutions. A classical membrane contactor modeling approach has been first proposed and successfully checked against a limited series of experimental data. In a second step, a large number of simulations has been performed based on this model in order to evaluate the different design characteristics (module length, packing factor) and the associated operating parameters (gas and liquid pressure drops) which enable a 90% CO2 capture ratio to be obtained on a flue gas containing 15% CO2. The possibility to potentially achieve intensification factors (i.e. relative volume of the unit compared to a standard packed column) up to a factor 11 has been verified. The conditions leading to this level of performances are however very specific and a great number of solutions do not provide an intensification effect or lead to unrealistic gas and/or liquid pressure drops. In order to extract the solutions which are physically and industrially feasible, a selection among the data set which offer an intensification factor ( I > 1), together with a low enough pressure drop on the gas side (<5000 Pa) and on liquid side (<10 5 Pa) has been performed.

texts.orig: -A different simulation strategy could have been performed, based for instance on a multivariable optimization study in order to identify the optimal domain for a fixed membrane contactor length. Optimization tools such as genetic algorithms would certainly be of interest in order to select the most efficient set of variables which maximizes the intensification factor (Corriou et al., 2008). Such an approach is clearly beyond the scope of this paper.

Optimization tools such as genetic algorithms would certainly be of interest in order to select the most efficient set of variables which maximizes the intensification factor (Corriou et al., 2008). texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: - A more refined modeling approach could be first investigated, in order to evaluate the incidence of the several simplifying hypothesis postulated. The role of hydrodynamic dispersion effects (i.e. gas and/or liquid flow which do not fulfill the plug flow assumption) appears to be of particular interest. This question, which remains largely unexplored for membrane contactor studies on the gas side, is of particular relevance for large scale membrane contactors with packing factors of 0.5 or more. The interplay of gas pressure drop with efficient dispersion conditions has also to be mentioned; for fixed bed design for instance, it is known that a minimal pressure drop has to be respected in order to ensure correct gas phase distribution. Similarly, the analysis of the incidence of non isothermal conditions, or the role of water evaporation would be of interest but they require additional experimental and modeling efforts.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -The extension of the analysis proposed in this study to other solvents proposed for CO2 capture would be also interesting, in order to estimate the intensification effect that membrane contactors can potentially offer compared to packed columns. The 11 factor identified in this simulation study cannot be considered as generic and a large variety of performance improvement can be expected depending, among others, on the solvent properties (viscosity, kinetics, solubility, etc.).

The 11 factor identified in this simulation study cannot be considered as generic and a large variety of performance improvement can be expected depending, among others, on the solvent properties (viscosity, kinetics, solubility, etc.).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -Finally, the design characteristics selection proposed in this study has been made on some arbitrary basis (especially the maximal tolerable pressure drop data). Different selection strategies could also be tested, with maximal liquid pressure drop ( /Delta1 PL ) lower than 10 5 Pa, in order to evaluate membrane materials which are less resistant to wetting (Franco et al., 2008). Moreover, a more rigorous analysis, taking into account the capital costs (i.e. module production) and the associated operating costs (i.e. energy requirement for gas and liquid flows), would open the scope to a complete optimization problem of the CO2 capture costs for membrane contactors units. This ultimate target requires however liable data on the module cost function, which, to our knowledge, are not available in the open literature.

texts.orig: Appendix A. Calculation of the overall mass transfer coefficient ( K )
texts.text: Appendix A. Calculation of the overall mass transfer coefficient ( K )

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall mass transfer coefficient has been computed according to a series of equations and variables data already recommended in previous studies (see for instance Abu-Zahra et al., 2007). For the mass transfer coefficient on the liquid side kL , given the laminar conditions which prevail inside the fiber, classical correlations for smooth tubes based on Graetz number and Sherwood number can be proposed. The influence of the chemical reaction on the mass transfer coefficient is taken into account thanks to an enhancement factor, noted E , which depends, among others, on the Hatta number Ha (Tobiesen et al., 2007).
texts.text: The overall mass transfer coefficient has been computed according to a series of equations and variables data already recommended in previous studies (see for instance Abu-Zahra et al., 2007). For the mass transfer coefficient on the liquid side kL , given the laminar conditions which prevail inside the fiber, classical correlations for smooth tubes based on Graetz number and Sherwood number can be proposed. The influence of the chemical reaction on the mass transfer coefficient is taken into account thanks to an enhancement factor, noted E , which depends, among others, on the Hatta number Ha (Tobiesen et al., 2007).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the gas side mass transfer coefficient kG , the situation is more complex. Different approaches have been indeed developed depending on the fiber bundle geometrical characteristics (Shen et al., 2010). A systematic analysis of differences between the numerous correlations which have been already proposed is beyond the scope of this study. Moreover, for CO2 absorption in a chemical solvent, the gas side mass transfer resistance is known to have little influence on the overall mass transfer resistance (Feron et al., 2002). Consequently, one classical correlation, already proposed by different authors has been used for that purpose (Li and Chen, 2005).
